revision.text:
[[ja:バナナ型神話]] {{ill|Banana-type myth|ja|バナナ型神話|User:Immanuelle/draft|lt=Banana-type myths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banana-type myth|qq=}} {{Refimprove|date=February 2023|qq=}} {{Original research|date=March 2023|qq=}} '''Banana-Type Myths''' (バナナ型神話) are a Category of origin myths centered around death and short lifespans, primarily found in {{ill|Southeast Asia|en|Southeast Asia|qid=Q11708|lt=Southeast Asia|1=Southeast Asia}} and {{ill|Papua New Guinea|en|Papua New Guinea|qid=Q691|lt=Papua New Guinea|1=Papua New Guinea}}. These myths typically feature bananas as a key symbolic item, which inspired Scottish social anthropologist {{ill|James_George_Frazer|en|James_George_Frazer|qid=Q151936|lt=Sir James George Frazer|1=James George Frazer}} (1854–1941) to name them as such.{{Cite book|year=1997|title=Japanese Mythology Dictionary|publisher=Daiwa Shobo|isbn=978-4-479-84043-5|pages=254-255|qq=}} The "banana-type myths" generally follow the structure described below: A deity presents humans with a choice between a stone and a banana. Humans choose the banana because it is edible, rejecting the inedible stone. The stone symbolizes immortality, while the banana—which decays after producing offspring—represents mortality. Because humans chose the banana, they are destined to die, just as bananas wither after bearing fruit.{{Cite book |author=Yamada Hitoshi |chapter=Origin Myths of Death in Southeast Asia and Oceania: Issues Regarding the Distribution of “Banana-Type” and “Shedding-Type” Myths |editor=Matsumura Kazuo|title=Myths of Life and Death|publisher=Riton|series=Religious Studies Anthology 9|date=June 2004|pages=113-129|isbn=978-4-947668-65-3|qq=}} == Sulawesi (Celebes) Myth of the Alfur People (Toraja People) == Initially, the heavens and the earth were close, and humans survived through gifts lowered from the sky by the creator deity using ropes. One day, the deity lowered a stone. Our first ancestors cried out to the deity: "What should we do with this stone? Please give us something else." The deity withdrew the stone and lowered a banana instead. Our first ancestors ran to eat the banana. Then, a voice from the heavens said: "Since you have chosen the banana, your lives will resemble that of the banana. When the banana plant bears children, the parent plant dies. Likewise, you will die, and your children will take your place. Had you chosen the stone, your lives would have been unchanging and immortal."{{Cite book|和書|author=大林太良|title=日本神話の起源|publisher=角川選書|pages=|qq=}} Sulawesi, formerly called Celebes during colonial times, is home to the {{ill|Alifuru people|en|Alifuru people|qid=Q783227|lt=Alfur people|1=Alifuru people}}, which translates to "non-Muslims" or "forest people." Among them, the {{ill|Torajan people|en|Torajan people|qid=Q290201|lt=Toraja people|1=Torajan people}} preserve similar myths. == Japanese Mythology == [[Japanese mythology]] also contains comparable tales, particularly in the section on [[Tenson kōrin]] (the descent of the Heavenly Grandchild). When the Heavenly Grandchild [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] descended, the earthly deity [[Ōyamatsumi]] offered his daughters, [[Iwanaga-hime]] (the elder) and [[Konohanasakuya-hime]] (the younger), in marriage. Ninigi rejected the unattractive Iwanaga-hime and married only the beautiful Konohanasakuya-hime. Iwanaga-hime symbolized long life, while Konohanasakuya-hime symbolized prosperity. By rejecting Iwanaga-hime, Ninigi doomed his descendants (the emperors) to mortality. Though bananas do not appear in this story, Iwanaga-hime’s name contains “rock” (stone), and her rejection led to short lifespans. Konohanasakuya-hime, whose name contains “flower,” corresponds to the ephemeral banana.{{Cite book|author=Obayashi Tario|year=1973|title=Origins of Japanese Myths|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|pages=223-230|qq=}} This tale is also compared to {{ill|Genesis|en|Genesis|qid=Q29244|lt=Genesis|1=Genesis}} 29, where {{ill|Jacob|en|Jacob|qid=Q289957|lt=Jacob|1=Jacob}} marries sisters {{ill|Leah|en|Leah|qid=Q128847|lt=Leah|1=Leah}} (unattractive and fertile) and {{ill|Rachel|en|Rachel|qid=Q207389|lt=Rachel|1=Rachel}} (beautiful but less fertile). == Alternate Version of Cain and Abel == In the 6th-century tale "The Struggle Between Adam, Eve, and Satan," Luluwa (beautiful) corresponds to the banana, while Aclemia (unattractive) corresponds to the stone. Cain and Abel had twin sisters. Cain was paired with Abel’s twin sister Aclemia, and Abel with Cain’s twin sister Luluwa. Cain objected, leading to conflict and eventually the murder of Abel. Seth (the replacement for Abel) married Aclemia, while Cain married Luluwa. Their descendants populated the earth, with Cain’s line moving east to the land of Nod. == Genesis: The Tree of Life and the Tree of Knowledge == {{ill|Genesis|en|Genesis|qid=Q29244|lt=Genesis|1=Genesis}} in the {{ill|Hebrew Bible|en|Hebrew Bible|qid=Q732870|lt=Hebrew Bible|1=Hebrew Bible}} contains a variation of banana-type myths. God placed two trees in {{ill|Eden|en|Eden|qid=Q224780|lt=Eden|1=Eden}}: the [[:en:Tree of Life|Tree of Life]] and the {{ill|Tree of Knowledge|en|Tree of Knowledge|qid=Q1177202|lt=Tree of Knowledge|1=Tree of Knowledge}} of good and evil. Humans were forbidden to eat from the Tree of Knowledge. However, tempted by the {{ill|serpent|en|serpent|qid=Q441892|lt=serpent|1=Serpent}}, they chose to eat its fruit, gaining knowledge but losing immortality. This myth aligns with the banana-type structure: humans chose knowledge over eternal life.
"And the LORD God commanded the man, saying, ‘Of every tree of the garden you may freely eat; but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.’" (Genesis 2:16-17)
== Correspondence Table == {|class="wikitable" |- ! Myth !! Immortality Symbol !! Mortality Symbol |- | Banana-Type Myth || Stone || Banana |- | Japanese Mythology || Rock (Iwanaga-hime) || Flower (Konohanasakuya-hime) |- | Greek Mythology || Bone || Meat |- | Genesis 3 || Tree of Life || Tree of Knowledge |- | Salt Pillar Myth || Looking Back (Salt Pillar) || Not Looking Back (Human Mortality) |- | Hainuwele Myth || Coconut (Unripe, Solid) || Coconut (Branch) |} == See Also == * {{ill|Christian views on poverty and wealth|en|Christian views on poverty and wealth|qid=Q2965834|lt=Christian views on poverty and wealth|1=Christian views on poverty and wealth}} * {{ill|Baopuzi|en|Baopuzi|qid=Q831242|lt=Bao Puzi|1=Baopuzi}} - A 4th-century Chinese text on alchemy stating, "Plants decay and return to soil over time. Therefore, immortality cannot derive from them. However, metals and stones endure through ages, and their medicines confer immortality." == References == {{Iwanaga Faith|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|バナナ型神話|version=104676221|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:バナナ型神話]] {{wikidata link|Q11327952}} [[Category:Religion in Indonesia]] [[Category:Bananas]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:死生観]] [[Category:Mythological archetypes]]
